coredata.dc:title: Functionalized carbon dots from zein biopolymer as a sensitive and selective fluorescent probe for determination of sumatriptan
coredata.dc:creator: Azizi, Behrooz
Farhadi, Khalil
Samadi, Naser
coredata.dc:description: Functionalizing the surface of nanomaterials with certain recognition units is a promising method for the fabrication of new fluorescent nanosensors for target analysis. In this work, a fluorescent nanosensor was successfully fabricated through conjugating 3,5-dinitro benzoyl chloride with raw carbon dots (CDs) which were first prepared from zein biopolymer and their characteristics have been evaluated using various techniques such as high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, FT-IR, UV‐Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectrophotometery and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The prepared DNB-CDs have been used to develop a new, sensitive, simple, cost effective and selective probe for detection of sumatriptan. The proposed method relies on fluorescence quenching of modified carbon dots in the presence of sumatriptan. Modified carbon dot showed maximum emission at 446 nm. Stern-Volmer was obeyed in the concentration range of 1–15 nM with desirable limit of detection (0.32 nM) and selectivity toward sumatriptan. Commercially available tablets and human urine samples were successfully analyzed to investigate practical applicability of the proposed sensor in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Zein
Sumatriptan
DNB
Fluorescent nanosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Coupling of prepared CDs with DNB
Fluorescence detection of SUM and the possible mechanism
Sensitivity and selectivity of fluorescence probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence emission spectra of the DNB-CDs under different excitation wavelengths with 10 nm increment.
HRTEM images of the synthesized CDs (a) and DNB-CDs (b) and the corresponding size distributions of the CDs (c) and the DNB-CDs (d).
UV–vis absorption of DNB (a), CDs (b), and DNB-CDs (c).
FT-IR spectra of DNB (a), CDs (b) and DNB-CDs (c).
Full survey XPS spectra (a) and the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (b) N1s (C) of synthesized CDs.
Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (a) and DNB-CDs (b) at the same concentration of SUM (5 nM) at 380 nm excitation wavelength.
The effect of pH values on the FL intensity of DNB-CDs (60 mg L−1) in the absence (F0) and presence (F) of SUM (8 nM).
Effect of the concentration of DNB capped CDs on the fluorescence intensity (2.0 mL of phosphate buffer at pH = 10, [SUM] = 8 nM).
HNMR spectra of SUM in the absence (a) and presence (b) of DNB-CDs.
Schematic illustration of the drug detection based on DNB-CDs fluorescence quenching.

(a) Effect of SUM concentration (0–20 nM) on the fluorescence intensity of DNB-CDs, Inset: the plot of SUM concentration vs. fluorescence intensity of DNB-CDs, Conditions: 2.0 mL PBS (pH = 10), 300 μL of DNB-CDs (60 mg mL−1), λex = 380 nm.
Comparison of this method with some currently spectrophotometric and spectroflouorimetric reported techniques for determination of SUM.
Effect of coexisting foreign species ([SUM] = 8 nM).
Determination results of SUM drug in human urine sample.
Determination results of SUM drug in tablet.

head.ce:title: Functionalized carbon dots from zein biopolymer as a sensitive and selective fluorescent probe for determination of sumatriptan

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Functionalizing the surface of nanomaterials with certain recognition units is a promising method for the fabrication of new fluorescent nanosensors for target analysis. In this work, a fluorescent nanosensor was successfully fabricated through conjugating 3,5-dinitro benzoyl chloride with raw carbon dots (CDs) which were first prepared from zein biopolymer and their characteristics have been evaluated using various techniques such as high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, FT-IR, UV‐Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectrophotometery and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The prepared DNB-CDs have been used to develop a new, sensitive, simple, cost effective and selective probe for detection of sumatriptan. The proposed method relies on fluorescence quenching of modified carbon dots in the presence of sumatriptan. Modified carbon dot showed maximum emission at 446 nm. Stern-Volmer was obeyed in the concentration range of 1–15 nM with desirable limit of detection (0.32 nM) and selectivity toward sumatriptan. Commercially available tablets and human urine samples were successfully analyzed to investigate practical applicability of the proposed sensor in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Sumatriptan (SUM) (Fig. 1 ), 1-[3-(2-Dimethylaminoethyl)-1H-indol-5-yl]-N-methyl-methanesulfonamide, is a synthetic drug of triptan class which is subscribed for prophylaxis and acute migraine attacks with or without aura [1]. It is a specific and selective 5-hydroxyl tryptamine receptor (5-HT1D) agonist and has no effect on the other 5HT receptors (5HT2-5 HT7). Biomedical researches have shown that excessive consumption of triptan-family drugs may have a variety of side effects such as tension of the neck, drowsiness, paralysis, seizures, high blood pressure, swelling of legs or arms, and feel of shakiness [2,3]. Due to vital and physiological importance of SUM, assay of this drug in various samples especially biological samples has attracted many pharmaceutical analysts. SUM has been verified by EP and USP and liquid chromatography has been suggested as a standard method for its measurement in biological fluids and pharmaceutical preparations. Therefore, different liquid chromatographic methods such as capillary LC-MS-MS [4], HPLC [4–6], HPTLC [7,8], UPLC [9], micellar electrokinetic chromatography [10] as well as conductometry [11], voltammetry [12–14], densitometry [15], UV–Vis spectrophotometry [16–19], capillary electrophoresis [20] have been reported in the literature. Furthermore, only a few colorimetric probes and one spectrofluorometric procedures based on modified silver and gold nanoparticles have been employed for determination of four triptan family drugs [21–23]. Although these approaches provide good sensitivity in some cases, most of them are complicated, expensive and need specific internal standards, complexing agents and poisonous solvents. On the other hand, the application of above mentioned methods for detection of triptan-family drugs are tedious, time consuming and unsuitable for in situ assay due to their low sensitivity. Therefore, the vacancy of sensitive, fast, and simple methods for the detection of these drug families is completely felt.

The application of fluorescent probes in monitoring systems is constantly increasing due to their simplicity, selectivity, and cost-effectiveness [24–26]. Carbon dots (CDs), a novel class of carbonaceous fluorescent nanomaterials with spherical shape and sizes of below 10 nm, have attracted much attention due to their extraordinary characteristics such as much readily available precursors, simple synthesis, very high optical and chemical stabilities, high quantum yield, easy surface-functionalization, desirable water-solubility, low toxicity, and high biocompatibility [27–29]. CD systems have been applied for the detection of a range of drugs and small molecules including melamine, amoxicillin, 2,4-dinitrophenol, tetracyclines, hydrogen peroxide, picric acid, and pentachlorophenol [30–36]. However, CD detection probes suffer from lack of special selectivity to target objects and this deficiency leads to their limited applications. To expand their applications and to solve this problem, modification of nanomaterials with highly specific recognition sites such as organic molecules and biomolecules has been distinguished. Accordingly, in the present work for the first time, we have prepared CDs from zein biopolymer (the main natural protein in corn) as carbon source through a simple green procedure and then functionalized it with conjugating 3,5-dinitro benzoyl chloride (DNB) to develop a new spectroflouorimetric sensor for detection of SUM in real samples. The main purpose of this work was to establish a relatively simple, selective, sensitive, and inexpensive spectrofluorometric method based on functionalized CDs for the detection of pure SUM in pharmaceutical and human urine samples. To the best of our knowledge, there is no report regarding the assay of SUM using CDs in literature.

All chemicals used were of analytical grade or of the highest purity available. A standard stock solution of SUM was prepared by dissolving certain amount of working standard drug. The resultant solution was diluted to desired volume with deionized water to prepare desirable concentration. Hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide were used to adjust the solution pH. Phosphate buffer solutions (PBSs) were prepared from monosodium and disodium salts of phosphate.

The absorption spectra of CDs were recorded on a double-beam T80+ UV–Vis spectrophotometer PG with a 1 cm quartz cell. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded using a FP-6500 Jasco fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan). Microscopic images were obtained by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL 2010F) at 200 KV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded in KBr pellets on a NEXUS 670 FT-IR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Perkin Elmer PHI 6000C ECSA system with monochromatic Al KR (1486.6 eV) irradiation. Freeze drying of the samples was carried out using an ALPHA 1-4 freeze dryer (CHRIST, Germany). 1H (300 MHz) NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker DRX-300 Avance spectrometer.

CDs were prepared with a very simple method without adding any reagents by direct pyrolysis of zein biopolymer as carbon source. Briefly, 0.05 g zein powder was placed into a 10 mL beaker and was heated at 120 °C for 10 min. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained product was titrated with 0.1 M NaOH (for further dissolution) with the stirring of the solution until a brown solution was acquired. Finally, the big particles in mixture were removed by centrifugation (2000 ×g, for 10 min). Freeze drying of the obtained supernatant for SEM and XRD tests and also for CD functionalizing reactions was carried out under vacuum conditions at −50 °C for 40 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coupling of prepared CDs with DNB
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1 g CDs was dissolved in the least possible volume of water and the diluted to 10 mL with acetonitrile. Then, a solution containing 0.23 g DNB in 10 mL acetonitrile was added to the CDs solution and heated for 30 min at 40 °C. The obtained solution was cooled down to room temperature and unreacted DNB was discarded through washing the resulted precipitate with acetonitrile. Finally, the precipitate as DNB-CDs was collected and dried in a vacuum oven.

The quantum yield of CDs and DNB-CDs were calculated by comparing the integrated photoluminescence (PL) intensities and absorbency values against the quinine sulfate (QY 54% in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4) as reference [37]. For this purpose, the fluorescence spectra of CDs were recorded at excitation of 330 nm and maintaining all absorbance levels under 0.1 at 330 nm to minimize the re-absorption effects. The quantum yield was calculated according to following equation:

where Φ is the quantum yield, m is the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs. absorbance, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and subscripts “st” and “x” denote standard and sample, respectively. The quantum yield of the CDs and DNB-CDs were obtained to be about 5.7% and 26%, respectively. Also, the fluorescence intensity variations and QY changes of as-prepared DNB-CDs were less than 2% during storage in a refrigerator (4 °C) for one month.

300 μL of DNB-CDs aqueous solution (60 mg L−1) and a certain amount of SUM were mixed in a 10 mL centrifuge tube. The mixture was diluted to 2 mL with 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 10) and vortexed thoroughly for 2 min. After a reaction period of 10 min, fluorescence spectra were recorded with excitation at 380 nm. The sensitivity of the probe to SUM was obtained using a series of SUM solutions with different concentrations ranging from 1 to 20 nM. Interference experiments were also performed in the absence and presence of SUM under experimental conditions.

In this work, pristine CDs were prepared by direct pyrolyzation of zein as carbon source to provide carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine capped CDs. Then, DNB-functionalized CDs were fabricated from the reaction of amine groups of CDs with acyl groups of DNB. The as-prepared DNB-CDs were highly water soluble. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of DNB-CDs excited at different wavelengths (320–430 nm) have been shown in Fig. 2 . As seen, by increasing excitation wavelength from 320 to 430 nm in 10 nm increments, the fluorescence (FL) intensities of DNB-CDs were decreased and peaks shifted to higher wavelengths. Tunable emission of CDs was fabricated based on size-dependent fluorescence characteristics of CDs or the presence of different emissive sites on the surfaces of CDs [38–40]. Particle size and morphology of raw CDs and DNB-CDs were evaluated by HRTEM (Fig. 3a, b). The images showed that DNB-CDs had narrow size distribution and desirable monodispersity in the range of 4 to 9 nm with the average size of 6.05 nm (Fig. 3d) which showed a small change compared to pristine CDs (4.89 nm, Fig. 3c). The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs and DNB, DNB-CDs are shown in Fig. 4 . The appeared absorption band of CDs at 343 nm was assigned to л → л* transition which shifted to low wavelengths when the surface was modified with DNB. In DNB-CDs, appearance of a new strong peak at 263 nm regarding aromatic π systems of phenyl moiety confirms the successful modification of CDs with DNB.

In order to evaluate the process of functionalizing CDs, FT-IR analysis was performed. The FT-IR spectra of CDs, DNB-CDs and DNB are shown in Fig. 5 . The observed peaks in the spectrum of CDs at 3461, 1700, 1150 and 2928 cm−1 were attributed to the characteristic absorption bands of OH, CO, CO and CH stretching mode, respectively. Also, the appeared peak at 1464 cm−1 was related to CN stretching vibration. The obvious peaks occurring at 3402 cm−1 and 1654 cm−1 in DNB-CDs spectrum were attributed the stretching vibration of amine (NH) and amide (NHCO) groups, respectively, confirmed the reaction between amino group of CDs and acyl group of DNB. Furthermore, the presence of nitro group of DNB on the structure of final product (DNB-CDs) confirmed by appearing two absorbance frequencies at 1347 and 1543 cm−1.

The surface composition and elemental analysis of DNB-CDs were studied with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). XPS survey spectra (Fig. 6a) showed a predominant C 1s peak at ca. 284 eV, N1s peak at ca. 404 eV, and O1s peak at ca. 536 eV. High resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 6b) showed three peaks at 284.5 eV, 286.6 eV, and 288.2 eV which were attributed to CC/CC, CO, and CO, respectively. N 1s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 6c) verified the presence of CNC, N(C)3, NH with peaks at ca. 398.5, 400, 401 eV, respectively. The mentioned above observations confirmed that CDs functionalized with hydroxyl, carboxylic, and amino groups were successfully synthesized through the conjugation reaction between DNB and CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of SUM and the possible mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Preliminary studies showed that the FL intensity of CDs and DNB-CDs is quenched in the presence of SUM. Fig. 7 shows the PL emission spectra of CDs and DNB-CDs in the absence and presence of 5 nM SUM at 380 nm excitation wavelength. As can be seen in Fig. 7b, fluorescence intensities were notably decreased for DNB-CDs in comparison with unmodified CDs (Fig. 7a) which revealed the potential application of modified CDs as nanosensors in SUM detection through fluorescence quenching. Therefore, to obtain the ideal performance of the proposed fluorescent sensor, parameters influencing FL intensity were optimized.

In order to obtain the optimum conditions for the detection of SUM, the effect of different pH values in the range of 4–11 was investigated on the FL intensity of DNB-CDs and for this purpose phosphate buffer solutions were employed to achieve desirable pHs. Fig. 8 shows the effect of pH on FL intensities of DNB-CDs before (Fo) and after (F) the addition of SUM. As can be seen, maximum quenching was obtained at pH 10 probably due to the high interaction of carboxylic group of DNB-CDs with amino group of drug. Therefore, further experiments were performed at pH 10.

The effect of different concentrations of DNB-CDs on fluorescence quenching by SUM was also investigated (Fig. 9 ). It is well established that CDs concentration affects the intensity of fluorescence. However, if the concentration was very low, FL intensity was also very low at a given emission slit which may reduce linear range. Due to increased number of CDs active sites, the number of luminophore particles was also increased and therefore, PL intensity of the solution reached maximum intensity in the presence of 60 mg L−1 CDs. However, when the concentration of CDs was increased beyond the optimum amount, PL intensity was declined due to increased opacity of solution samples which resulted in the multiplication of light scattering. On the other hand, high concentrations of carbon quantum dots may self-quench CDs fluorescence [41]. Therefore, 60 mg L−1 was chosen as optimum concentration of CDs for the determination of SUM.

As shown in Fig. 7, the amount of FL quenching of DNB-CDs was greater than that of unmodified CDs in the presence of similar concentrations of SUM. It seems this clear difference in quenching is related to the interaction of nitro groups of DNB-CDs with amino group of SUM via hydrogen bonding. To provide further information for this claim, the H-NMR spectra of SUM in the presence and absence of DNB-CDs were recorded. The resulting spectra are shown in Fig. 10 . As can be seen, the appeared peak at 10.89 ppm regarding resonance of hydrogen atom linked to nitrogen adjacent sulfonamide group of SUM shifts to weaker field (ca. 11 ppm) in the presence of functionalized carbon dots. This fact confirms that the quenching is a result of hydrogen bond formation between the nitro groups of DNB-CDs and aliphatic –NH moiety on phenyl aromatic ring of SUM. Moreover, no significant shift in peak location (ca. 4.34 ppm) regarding the proton of –NH on pyrrole ring of SUM after adding DNB-CDs confirms the proposed new mechanism illustrated in Fig. 11 .

FTIR spectroscopy of DNB-CDs, SUM (drug) and those mixtures was evaluated to confirm the proposed strategy detection (Fig. S1, supplementary materials). Usually, hydrogen bond formation affects the vibration frequency and electron density of related groups through a decrease in the absorption peak intensity of finger printing area [42]. Similar to reports in literature, the peaks intensity of finger print area in mixture containing DNB-CDs and SUM decreases in comparison with those alone. On the other hand, remarkable decrease in absorption peak intensity of nitro groups in finger prints area (about 1350–1550 cm−1) as well as a shift to low wavenumbers confirms that the hydrogen bonding forms through nitro groups of DNB-CDs.

FTIR spectroscopy of DNB-CDs, SUM (drug) and those mixtures was evaluated to confirm the proposed strategy detection (Fig. S1, supplementary materials). Usually, hydrogen bond formation affects the vibration frequency and electron density of related groups through a decrease in the absorption peak intensity of finger printing area [42]. Similar to reports in literature, the peaks intensity of finger print area in mixture containing DNB-CDs and SUM decreases in comparison with those alone. On the other hand, remarkable decrease in absorption peak intensity of nitro groups in finger prints area (about 1350–1550 cm−1) as well as a shift to low wavenumbers confirms that the hydrogen bonding forms through nitro groups of DNB-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of proposed DNB-CDs as SUM probe is also examined against some conventional cations and biological molecules at a concentration of 80 nM while the concentration of SUM was 8 nM. The percent of change in fluorescence intensity of SUM probe in the absence and presence of tested interferences was calculated. The obtained values were summarized in Table 2 . From these results, it is demonstrated that a relative wide range of presented species do not interfere even at concentrations of 10 times higher than those of SUM as a target analyte and the constructed SUM probe based DNB-CDs are not significantly sensitive to other species under similar conditions.

In this work, we synthesized multifunctional (NH2, OH, COOH) CDs from zein with simple solvent-free direct pyrolyzation. Then, raw CDs were successfully decorated with DNB in order to fabricate a novel nanosensor for selective detection of sumatriptan. The prepared DNB-CDs showed selective response to SUM drug without the interference of other potential interfering substances. The proposed luminescent sensor was based on fluorescence quenching of SUM which interacted with DNB functionalized CDs via hydrogen bonding. Under optimum conditions, little or no interference was observed from any material normally coexist with SUM drug. Therefore, this method could be applied for the detection of SUM drug at trace levels. In addition to its good sensitivity and low detection limit (LOD = 0.32 nM), other advantages such as simplicity, rapidity, and high selectivity were also observed. High selectivity was assumed that SUM could fit well the defect of the DNB-CDs surface and could interacted strongly with DNB. Also, a comparison between the figures of merit of this luminescent CDs probe with some previously reported methods obviously revealed that the proposed fluorescent sensor could be considered as an efficient SUM probe.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
FTIR spectra of DNB-CDs (a) and SUM (b). (c) Fourier transform infrared spectrometry of mixture of DNB-CDs and SUM.
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2019.02.026.
